findings: Roughly 8 to 15% of SWIFT wires to Chinese suppliers arrive short, delayed, or returned. The two biggest causes: intermediary bank fee deductions and beneficiary name mismatches.

Most banks default to SHA (shared fees) in SWIFT field 71A. SHA does not actually mean shared evenly: it means you pay your bank's send fee, the supplier eats every correspondent bank deduction in the chain.

Each correspondent bank in the chain deducts $15 to $50 directly from the wire amount before passing it on. The sender is never notified. 1 to 3 hops is typical for a US to China wire, so on a $50,000 wire a supplier can lose $30 to $150 invisibly, sometimes more.
Real reference: a Vienna company paid $12,000 to a US vendor using SHA. Vendor received $11,940. The $60 vanished into the correspondent chain with no line item.

The fix is one of two things. Either change the SWIFT instruction to OUR (sender pays all chain fees, supplier gets full amount, costs the sender $30 to $75 more upfront) or bypass the SWIFT chain entirely via CIPS or MYbank routing, where there is no intermediary chain to deduct from.

China-bound wires also fail on beneficiary name mismatch. A 5 to 10% return rate in the China corridor traces back to pinyin vs Chinese character name, missing 'Co., Ltd.', or wrong company-form suffix on the SWIFT MT103. Returns cost 2 to 3 weeks of delay and another round of fees.

angles.video_title: Your supplier just asked for the $200 they did not get
angles.hook: You paid $50,000. Your supplier says $49,800 landed. You are not crazy and they are not lying.
angles.body: Here is what happened. Your wire went through SWIFT, the global bank messaging network. SWIFT does not move money on its own. Real banks in the middle, called correspondent banks, pass the funds hop by hop. Most US to China wires hit 1 to 3 of them. Each one is allowed to deduct its own fee, usually $15 to $50, straight from the amount before passing it on. Your bank never warns you. Your supplier just sees a smaller number arrive. That $200 gap is not a scam. It is the chain.
angles.close: Ask your bank to send as OUR, not SHA. Or skip the chain entirely.
angles.visual: Wire receipt showing $50,000 sent. Three middle banks each shaving $50, $35, $80 in animation. Final number landing at $49,835. Supplier WhatsApp message: 'short 165, please top up'.

angles.video_title: SHA, OUR, BEN. The three letters that decide who eats the fees
angles.hook: Every SWIFT wire has a hidden three-letter code. It decides who pays the chain.
angles.body: On the wire form, there is a box called details of charges. Three options. SHA, which most banks default to, means shared. Sounds fair, is not. You pay your bank's send fee, your supplier eats every correspondent deduction down the chain. OUR means sender pays everything. You front the intermediary cost, your supplier gets the full invoice amount. BEN means beneficiary pays everything. Your supplier loses your bank's fee plus the chain plus their receiving fee. On a $50,000 wire, the gap between OUR and BEN can be $100 to $300 on the supplier side.
angles.close: Default to OUR when you want your supplier to receive the exact invoice. Most banks will not tell you this exists.
angles.visual: Three boxes side by side. SHA: sender icon takes one coin, supplier takes 3 coins. OUR: sender takes 4 coins, supplier takes zero. BEN: sender takes zero, supplier takes 5 coins. Highlight: 'OUR = supplier gets full amount'.

angles.video_title: Why your wire arrived 3 days late and short $150
angles.hook: Your wire was supposed to clear in 2 days. Day 5, your supplier is still chasing.
angles.body: Two reasons it stalls. One, intermediary banks. A US to China SWIFT wire usually routes through 1 to 3 correspondent banks. Each one takes a business day to process and shaves $15 to $50 in fees. Two, compliance review in China. Chinese banks check the beneficiary name on the wire against the company registration. If the name on your wire says 'Shenzhen ABC Trading' but the registered name is 'Shenzhen ABC Trading Co., Ltd.', the wire pauses for manual review, sometimes for 3 to 10 days, sometimes returned to sender entirely.
angles.close: Two minutes spent matching the exact registered name saves a week of supplier emails.
angles.visual: Calendar with 5 days crossed out. Day 1 sender bank. Day 2 correspondent A. Day 3 correspondent B. Day 4 Chinese bank compliance. Day 5 supplier finally sees it. Each step shows a fee deduction.

angles.video_title: The supplier name typo that costs you 2 weeks
angles.hook: You typed your supplier's name from their email. The wire bounced. Here is why.
angles.body: Chinese banks match the beneficiary name on the SWIFT wire against the registered company name on file with the bank, character for character. Common mistakes that bounce wires back. One, you wrote the pinyin name when the bank has the Chinese character name. Two, you left off 'Co., Ltd.' when the registered name includes it. Three, your supplier gave you their trading name but the bank account is in the holding company's name. Beneficiary mismatch is the number one reason wires to China get returned, around 5 to 10% in the corridor. Return time, 2 to 3 weeks. Cost, two fee cycles plus your supplier's patience.
angles.close: Before every wire, ask the supplier to send the exact name on the bank account, copy paste it.
angles.visual: Side by side: 'Shenzhen ABC Trading' vs 'Shenzhen ABC Trading Co., Ltd.' Red X on left, green tick on right. Bottom of frame: 'this single typo = 14 day delay'.

angles.video_title: The wire shortfall problem only exists on SWIFT
angles.hook: Wire arrived short? That problem only exists when you route through SWIFT.
angles.body: Here is the structural difference. SWIFT is a messaging network connecting 11,000 banks. The money still has to travel through real banks one by one to get there, and each one can shave a fee. CIPS, which is China's Cross-border Interbank Payment System, and MYbank, which is Ant Group's bank running local CNY rails, both bypass that chain. There are no correspondents to deduct from. No SHA vs OUR fee math. The amount you send is the amount that lands. CIPS settles in about 300 milliseconds. MYbank delivers same-day for 80% of payments, next-day for 90%.
angles.close: If your supplier is in China and you do not need a wire chain, do not use one.
angles.visual: Two routes drawn on a map. Route A SWIFT: 4 bank icons in a chain, each shaving a coin. Route B CIPS / MYbank: 1 arrow direct, full amount lands. Labels: 'short payment risk: high' vs 'short payment risk: zero'.

findings: SWIFT (Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunication) is messaging only. It tells banks what to do, then real correspondent banks move the money hop by hop, 1 to 3 hops typical for US/EU to China.
SWIFT speed to China: 1 to 5 business days. Best case 1 day for direct correspondent. Typical 2 to 4 days for USD to CNY conversion. 4 to 10 days when compliance flags trigger.

CIPS (Cross-border Interbank Payment System) is China's RMB clearing rail. Direct settlement, no intermediary chain. Average settlement 300 milliseconds. Connects 1,600+ participants across 180+ countries as of 2026.

MYbank, an Ant Group bank, runs local CNY rails into the Chinese banking system. 80% of supplier payments clear same-day, 90% next-day. Cut-off window: 9:30 to 15:00 China Standard Time, payments outside the window roll to next business day.
WorldFirst's 1688 World Pay sends CNY direct to 1688 and Taobao domestic suppliers via MYbank routing, 0.8% transfer fee, no SWIFT intermediary deductions, no SHA/OUR/BEN fee math.

Speed comparison on a real $50,000 supplier payment: SWIFT through a US bank: 3 to 5 business days, $750 to $2,000 total cost including FX margin. CIPS/MYbank via WorldFirst: same-day or next-day, around $150 to $300 all-in.

angles.video_title: SWIFT vs CIPS vs MYbank in 60 seconds
angles.hook: There are three ways to get money to a Chinese supplier. Most operators only know one.
angles.body: SWIFT, the global bank messaging network, is what your bank uses by default. 1 to 5 days, 1 to 3 intermediary banks each shaving fees, supplier often gets less than invoiced. CIPS, China's Cross-border Interbank Payment System, is the RMB clearing rail. Direct settlement, no intermediaries, average 300 milliseconds. MYbank, Ant Group's bank, runs the last-mile CNY rails into Chinese supplier accounts. 80% same-day, 90% next-day, no chain fees. The first option is the slowest and the most expensive. The other two are why specialised providers exist.
angles.close: Most banks only offer option one. Your supplier prefers options two and three.
angles.visual: Three columns. SWIFT: 1 to 5 days, 3 hops, fees deducted. CIPS: 300ms, direct, no hops. MYbank: same-day, direct, no hops. Animation shows a wire travelling each route at different speeds.

angles.video_title: Why your supplier asks 'are you sending via 1688 or wire'
angles.hook: Your supplier asks how you are sending the money. There is a reason.
angles.body: From their side, a SWIFT wire means an unpredictable wait. 3 days, 5 days, sometimes a week. The amount that lands could be 30 to 200 USD less than your invoice because of correspondent fees they cannot see in advance. Then a top-up email. Then production delayed. A 1688 platform payment or a same-day CNY rail means the full amount lands today or tomorrow, in their account, in CNY, no fee math. Production starts immediately. That is why suppliers ask. It is not just about the money. It is about how predictable their week looks.
angles.close: The rail you pick changes your supplier's experience as much as the price.
angles.visual: Supplier WhatsApp screen: 'send via 1688 or wire transfer?' Then split screen: SWIFT route showing 'wait 5 days, top up $150 missing' vs 1688 route showing 'received in full, production starting tomorrow'.

angles.video_title: The 300 millisecond payment your bank does not offer
angles.hook: CIPS settles supplier payments in 300 milliseconds. Your bank takes 5 days. Why.
angles.body: CIPS, China's Cross-border Interbank Payment System, is a real-time clearing rail purpose-built for RMB. Direct settlement between participating banks. No correspondent chain. No SHA vs OUR fee math. Average settlement: 300 milliseconds. 1,600 participating banks across 180 countries. Your bank does not offer it because your bank is not on CIPS as a direct participant. They route through SWIFT instead, hop by hop, day by day. Specialised cross-border providers integrate with CIPS or MYbank directly, which is why a wire that takes your bank 5 days can clear through them same-day.
angles.close: Same destination. Different rail. 4 days saved.
angles.visual: Clock animation: SWIFT bar fills slowly over 5 days. CIPS bar fills in a millisecond flash. Both reach the same supplier. 'Why the gap: your bank is not on CIPS.' Caption underneath.

angles.video_title: Speed test: $50K to a Chinese supplier, 3 rails
angles.hook: Same $50,000. Three rails. Three completely different days for your supplier.
angles.body: Rail one: SWIFT through your local bank. 3 to 5 business days. $750 to $2,000 total cost, including 1 to 4% FX margin and 1 to 3 intermediary deductions. Supplier sometimes gets less than $50,000. Rail two: CIPS via a specialised provider. Same-day or next-day. Around $200 to $400 all-in, no intermediary chain. Rail three: MYbank routing for 1688 suppliers, 0.8% transfer fee, 80% same-day, 90% next-day. Supplier sees the full amount in CNY in their domestic Chinese bank account. Same destination, same money, three completely different experiences.
angles.close: Your supplier does not care which rail you use. Until they do.
angles.visual: Three timeline bars. SWIFT: 5 days, $1,500 fees, $49,850 lands. CIPS: 1 day, $300 fees, $49,700 lands. MYbank: same-day, $400 fees, full $50K lands in CNY. Numbers fade in next to each bar.

angles.video_title: SWIFT was built for banks. CIPS was built for trade.
angles.hook: SWIFT was built in 1973 for banks talking to each other. CIPS was built for actually paying suppliers.
angles.body: SWIFT solved one problem in 1973: how do banks send each other messages. It never solved who moves the money. So real banks in between still pass it hand to hand, deducting fees each time. CIPS, launched by the People's Bank of China in 2015, skipped that whole problem. It is one network that clears and settles RMB payments directly, in real time. No middlemen. No fee chain. Trade flows between Asia and the rest of the world were the use case, not bank-to-bank correspondence. That is why a CIPS payment behaves nothing like a SWIFT wire, even when the sender and the recipient are the same.
angles.close: Two networks. Two design eras. Two completely different experiences.
angles.visual: Two timelines. 1973: SWIFT logo with old telex machine. 2015: CIPS logo with sleek server racks. Below, side-by-side flow diagrams: SWIFT zigzags through 3 banks, CIPS goes one straight line.
